data.title: NLIGHT outlines 40% A&D revenue growth target for 2025 while advancing defense laser programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: nLIGHT, Inc. (LASR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott H. Keeney opened the call by highlighting a strong quarter for nLIGHT, with revenue, gross margin, and adjusted EBITDA all ahead of expectations. He stated that "results were primarily driven by our record aerospace and defense products and development revenue," with A&D revenue making up 66% of total sales in the quarter. Keeney described the company's position as "well positioned to drive continued growth in A&D with leading high-power laser technology developed over the past 2 decades across the entire technology stack from chips to full laser systems, which is supported by our U.S. manufacturing sites."
  • Keeney reported solid progress on the HELSI-2 program, a $171 million Department of Defense effort for a 1-megawatt high-energy laser, with shipment of critical components being a key driver of record defense product revenue. He also noted the near completion of work on the Army's DE M-SHORAD program and active engagement with new opportunities under the President's Golden Dome executive order focused on non-kinetic missile defense capabilities. Internationally, Keeney mentioned that "in the second quarter, we began shipping to a new international customer, and we have a growing pipeline of new global opportunities."
  • On the commercial side, Keeney indicated that revenue was ahead of expectations, driven by increases in both microfabrication and industrial. However, he cautioned, "we do not expect a sustained improvement in overall demand, particularly with respect to the majority of our industrial applications, and we continue to rationalize our investments in our commercial markets."
  • CFO Joseph Corso stated, "Our second quarter results were characterized by strong execution across the board. Better-than-expected revenue, a strong mix of business and higher factory absorption, coupled with exceptional execution from our manufacturing and operations teams drove meaningful upside to our gross margin."

Outlook

  • Management expects sequential growth in A&D revenue throughout the rest of 2025, with Keeney expressing "increased confidence that we can grow our revenues in aerospace and defense by at least 40% in 2025."
  • Revenue guidance for Q3 2025 is set in the range of $62 million to $67 million, with a midpoint of $64.5 million. Product revenue is expected to be approximately $45 million and development revenue $19 million.
  • Product gross margin for Q3 is expected in the range of 32% to 36%, while development gross margin is anticipated at approximately 8%, resulting in a total gross margin range of 24% to 30%. Adjusted EBITDA is guided in the range of $2 million to $6 million for the third quarter.

Financial Results

  • Q2 2025 total revenue was $61.7 million, an increase of 22% year-over-year and up 19.5% from Q1 2025. Aerospace and Defense revenue reached a record $40.7 million, up 48.6% year-over-year and 24% sequentially. Defense products revenue grew 74.5% year-over-year.
  • Commercial markets revenue was $21 million, down 9% year-over-year but up 11% sequentially. The increase was attributed to pent-up demand in microfabrication following the manufacturing transition out of China.
  • Total gross margin was 29.9%, products gross margin 38.5%, and development gross margin 13.1%.
  • GAAP net loss was $3.6 million or ($0.07) per share, compared to a net loss of $8.1 million or ($0.16) per share last quarter. Non-GAAP net income was $2.9 million or $0.06 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA stood at $5.6 million.
  • The company ended the quarter with $114 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and investments.

Q&A

  • Daniel James Eggerichs, Craig-Hallum: Asked what drove A&D outperformance and margin strength. CFO Corso responded that "we just executed very well in the quarter," citing amplifier sales into the HELSI-2 program as a key factor and attributing margin outperformance to "a handful of small things that this quarter all went very well."
  • Eggerichs: Inquired about laser sensing programs and revenue outlook. Corso explained that existing platforms saw higher volumes due to customer restocking and that new, classified programs are progressing, with expected LRIP phase work in the second half.
  • Eggerichs: Asked about product gross margin outlook given Q2’s high 38.5% result. Corso said, "as we forecast going forward, right, we're not going to forecast that upside. Could that happen again? Yes, but that's not something that we're banking on nor do I think that as you're building your model, you should bank on."
  • James Andrew Ricchiuti, Needham: Sought views on 2026 and drivers of the 2025 outlook increase. Corso stated it is "a little early for us to really start talking about 2026" but cited broad-based growth in defense portfolio and HELSI-2 as contributors to the 2025 outlook.
  • Ruben Roy, Stifel: Asked if the increase in the A&D growth outlook to 40% was driven by HELSI-2. Corso confirmed, "there was an improvement on that end of it," noting capacity increases and knock-on effects for development work.
  • Rodney McFall, Northcoast Research: Asked about the international customer opportunity and execution risk. CEO Keeney described the opportunity as "material" and "all still in the early stages." McFall also asked about commercial market rationalization; Keeney explained that engineers are being shifted from industrial lasers to new defense-based lasers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on drivers of outperformance, the sustainability of high margins, and specifics around new business, reflecting a slightly positive but probing tone. There was interest in the durability of A&D growth and execution on new programs.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Keeney stating "we are well positioned" and Corso highlighting "exceptional execution." In Q&A, responses were measured and transparent, with Corso saying, "we're not going to forecast that upside" and providing context on program transitions and risk.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more confident, particularly regarding the outlook for A&D growth and operational execution. Analysts remained constructive but sought additional clarification on sustainability and program timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for A&D revenue growth increased from "at least 25%" in Q1 to "at least 40%" in Q2, with the HELSI-2 program cited as a key driver.
  • Gross margin rose from 26.7% in Q1 to 29.9% in Q2, with product gross margin moving from 33.5% to 38.5%.
  • Sequential revenue growth was higher this quarter for both A&D and commercial segments.
  • Management’s confidence grew, with greater emphasis on broad-based defense growth and successful execution on program transitions. Analysts’ questions remained focused on growth drivers and risk, consistent with prior quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged risks related to transitioning amplifier products from R&D to manufacturing, with Corso noting "this transition, while not without risk, is critical as we continue optimizing our amplifier production line for higher volumes."
  • CEO Keeney highlighted uncertainty in commercial demand and ongoing rationalization of investments in those markets.
  • The company continues to monitor execution risk with new international customers and program transitions. There was no reference to tariff risk in the current quarter’s transcript.

Final Takeaway

nLIGHT delivered a strong second quarter with record A&D revenue driven by the HELSI-2 program, improved gross margins, and better-than-expected results across product and development lines. Management raised A&D revenue growth expectations for 2025 to at least 40%, supported by a growing pipeline of defense programs and successful operational transitions. While commercial market demand remains uncertain and program scaling carries risk, the company is focusing resources on its core defense opportunities and expects continued sequential growth through the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oxford Lane Capital estimates NAV per share at $4.13-$4.23 as of July 31
data.description:
• Oxford Lane Capital (NASDAQ:OXLC) announced on Friday an unaudited estimated net asset value per share in the range of $4.13 to $4.23 as of July 31.
  • The stock was trading +0.93% pre-market to $3.27.
  • As of July 31, the closed-end management investment company had ~485.9M shares of common stock issued and outstanding.

data.title: Ford delays the launch of a new electric truck and van model to 2028 as it plays small ball
data.description:
Ford Motor (NYSE:F) confirmed that it is delaying the launch of two next-generation electric vehicles. A full-size electric pickup truck aimed as the successor to the F-150 Lightning and the next E-Transit van will not be made until 2028. 

The delay is due to its new focus on developing smaller, more affordable electric vehicles, reacting to increased competition and a slowdown in U.S. demand for large, high-priced EVs. Notably, Ford (NYSE:F) has struggled to make the current electric vehicle lineup profitable, with losses exceeding $1 billion in the last reported quarter and a projected $5.5 billion annual loss for its EV division in 2025,

The models have seen their production timeline pushed back multiple times already. 

Looking ahead, Ford (F) thinks its strategic pivot can help it deliver products that better match consumer demand and improve the profitability and competitiveness of its electric vehicle lineup.

Shares of Ford (F) edged 0.4% higher in premarket trading. The auto stock is up close to 14% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Lamar Advertising stock slides as less-than-hoped for Q2 revenue growth disappoints
data.description:
Lamar Advertising (NASDAQ:LAMR) stock dropped 3.8% in Friday premarket trading after the advertising billboard REIT trimmed its full-year guidance as Q2 revenue missed the Wall Street consensus, indicating that revenue growth may not be as strong as previously expected. 

"Our pacings indicate further year-over-year improvement in revenues is likely in the second half of 2025, though perhaps not to the degree that we'd anticipated entering the year," CEO Sean Reilly said.

The owner and operator of billboards now expects full-year adjusted FFO per share of $8.10-$8.20, down from the $81.3-8.28 range it issued in February and lower than the S&P CapIQ consensus of $8.27.

Q2 AFFO per share of $2.22, beating the average analyst estimate of $2.18, rose from $2.08 in last year's Q2.

Q2 revenue of $579.3M, trailing the $580.8M consensus, increased from $565.3M in the same period a year ago. 

Total operating expenses of $381.6M, compared with $381.0M in the year-ago quarter.

Adjusted EBITDA of $278.4M, just above the $278.3M consensus, increased from $271.6M a year ago.

Total capital expenditures jumped to $38.2M from $22.6M in last year's Q2.

Conference call at 9:00 AM ET.

data.title: BuzzFeed reaffirms $195M–$210M revenue guidance while expanding direct audience and AI-native platform strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BuzzFeed, Inc. (BZFD) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jonah Peretti highlighted the company's continued transformation, stating, "We've been focused for the past 2 years on transforming our business, and these efforts are continuing to bear fruit." He emphasized that the company has shifted to direct and diversified audience sources, with "Direct visits, internal referrals and app usage now make up 61% of BuzzFeed's O&O traffic, surpassing Facebook and reducing dependence on external algorithmic platforms." Peretti noted, "Engagement and loyalty are rising. Nearly half of BuzzFeed and HuffPost daily users return more than once a week. Log-in users on buzzfeed.com have tripled over the past 2 years."
  • He described the company's strategy to expand scalable, tech-enabled business lines, citing a 38% year-over-year growth in gross sales of non-Amazon merchants, and a 55% increase for the BuzzFeed brand alone. Programmatic advertising grew by 11% year-over-year. He also discussed BuzzFeed Studios' growth, noting, "Our feature film with Lionsgate, F Marry Kill was the #1 movie on Hulu in June."
  • Peretti introduced the BF Island app, calling it "the first AI-native social media platform" and stating, "We are expanding our beta testing group and we will open up to select BuzzFeed community members by the end of Q3."
  • CFO Matthew Omer remarked, "Our Q2 '25 results reflect the strength and the resilience of our diversified business model as well as the benefits of the transformation work we've done over the past 2 years." Omer also stressed a focus on "a leaner, more efficient organization and prioritize high-margin revenue streams, positioning BuzzFeed for long-term value creation."

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its full year 2025 guidance: "revenue in the range of $195 million to $210 million and adjusted EBITDA between $10 million and $20 million, in line with the outlook we provided in March" (CFO Matthew Omer).
  • Omer stated, "We expect continued growth in programmatic advertising and affiliate commerce to lead our revenue mix, while content will vary based on the timing of studio projects and the stabilization of our direct sold business."
  • Management reiterated that Q4 is historically significant for revenue, and "the first half of the year have been very encouraging with meaningful progress across our core KPIs."

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for the quarter was $46.4 million compared to $41.1 million in Q2 '24, an increase of 13% year-over-year (CFO Matthew Omer).
  • Studio revenue increased nearly fourfold, content revenue grew 53% to $10.7 million, and commerce and other revenues rose to $13.1 million.
  • Advertising revenue was $22.6 million, with programmatic advertising up 11% to $17.4 million, offset by a 31% decline in direct sold advertising.
  • Net loss from continuing operations was ($10.6 million), primarily driven by a $5.5 million nonrecurring charge related to the extinguishment of convertible notes. Adjusted EBITDA was $2 million, including a onetime $2.4 million reversal.
  • For the first half of 2025, total revenue reached $82.4 million; net loss from continuing operations was ($23.1 million) and adjusted EBITDA losses improved to ($3.9 million).

Q&A

  • Juliana Clifton asked about diversification away from traditional referral sources. CEO Jonah Peretti responded, "Over 60% of our traffic...comes from direct sources, so internal referrals and app usage. We've really transformed the company over the past few years to be much more about things like homepage traffic...having a stronger, more direct audience relationship is really the key to building a strong digital media business."
  • On platform dependency, Peretti said, "It's become clear recently that...these kinds of products are overoptimized...We're seeing that increasingly, there's a demand for people who want to get off their phones...BuzzFeed, HuffPost and Tasty are really benefiting from being trusted destinations."
  • Regarding BuzzFeed Island, Peretti stated, "It's incredibly fun and can't wait to share with more people."
  • Asked which platforms show promise, Peretti pointed to "Apple News" for HuffPost and stressed the value of direct and homepage traffic, as well as the importance of differentiated IP for cross-platform reach and monetization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ questions focused on traffic sources and platform dependency, with a neutral tone and an interest in strategic direction rather than skepticism.
  • Management's sentiment was confident, with CEO Peretti describing user engagement and strategic transformation as successful and using phrases such as, "I can't wait to share it with more people as we expand our beta testing and build towards a public launch."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more assertive confidence about both operational improvements and the rollout of new initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s guidance range for revenue and adjusted EBITDA remained unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus sharpened on direct audience growth and proprietary platform development, with more emphasis on reducing platform dependency in Q2.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from macro conditions and programmatic revenue resilience in Q1 to direct audience relationships and new platforms in Q2.
  • Programmatic advertising, studio, and commerce revenue lines demonstrated stronger growth in Q2 versus Q1, while direct sold advertising and content continued to decline.
  • Management’s confidence and language about innovation and new platform development increased from Q1 to Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Matthew Omer mentioned that content revenue "will vary based on the timing of studio projects and the stabilization of our direct sold business."
  • The company continues to face market softness in direct sold advertising and content, acknowledged as "muted demand in this category and our deliberate move towards higher-value partnerships."
  • Omer reminded that revenue is seasonal and "dependent on the strength of Q4 performance as is typical for our industry."

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that its strategy of shifting to direct audience relationships, diversifying revenue sources, and investing in new AI-driven platforms such as BF Island is driving measurable progress. The company reaffirmed its full year guidance, highlighted substantial revenue and engagement growth in scalable business lines, and signaled confidence in the ability to deliver long-term value while continuing to adapt to changes in the digital media landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kingsway signals accelerated M&A with new capital, targets 3–5 acquisitions per year amid KSX momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kingsway Financial Services Inc. (KFS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John Taylor-Maloney Fitzgerald, CEO, announced that "the second quarter of 2025 marked a major inflection point for the company." He emphasized Kingsway's unique position as "the only publicly traded U.S. company employing the search fund model to acquire and build great businesses." Highlighting a strategic shift, Fitzgerald stated, "our Board of Directors, with management support, made an exciting decision: we are ready to accelerate growth."
  • Fitzgerald reported a private placement (PIPE transaction) with five institutional investors, contributing $15.7 million. He explained that this capital, combined with cash flow and other sources, will "provide Kingsway with the financial resources to scale faster and to deliver the company's multiyear growth ambitions."
  • The company raised its KSX acquisition target from 2–3 to 3–5 per year, citing increased confidence in the model and a growing pipeline.
  • Since completing the PIPE, Fitzgerald detailed the execution of three acquisitions: Roundhouse Electric & Equipment Co. for $22.4 million, AAA Flexible Pipe Cleaning Corp (Advanced Plumbing and Drain) for up to $5 million, and The HR Team as a tuck-in for Ravix Group. Each acquisition aligns with the capital-light, recurring revenue criteria central to Kingsway's strategy.
  • Fitzgerald reported that Kingsway's trailing 12-month adjusted run rate EBITDA for the businesses owned as of quarter end stands at approximately $22 million to $23 million.
  • Kent A. Hansen, CFO, stated, "For the second quarter, consolidated revenue was $30.9 million, an increase of 16.9% compared to $26.4 million in the second quarter of 2024." He added, "Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $1.7 million for the 3 months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $2.5 million in the prior year quarter."
  • Hansen reported KSX segment revenue of $13.3 million, up 42.1% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA of $2.4 million, up 31%. He cited both organic growth and recent acquisitions as drivers.
  • Hansen addressed the Extended Warranty segment: "Revenue increased by 3.1% to $17.6 million in the second quarter, up from $17.1 million in the prior year period. Adjusted EBITDA was down to $600,000 from $1.6 million in the prior year quarter."
  • Hansen disclosed a $600,000 expense tied to a legal settlement, clarifying "this expense will not recur going forward."

Outlook

  • Fitzgerald stated, "We currently have 2 Operators-in-Residence, or OIRs, who are actively searching for our next acquisition targets, and we are in the process of interviewing high-quality candidates to expand this bench."
  • The company increased its target range for KSX acquisitions per year to 3–5 and has acquired 5 asset-light services businesses year-to-date.
  • No explicit forward-looking financial guidance was provided in the transcript.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for Q2 2025 was $30.9 million, up 16.9% year-over-year.
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $1.7 million for the quarter, compared to $2.5 million in the prior year period.
  • KSX segment revenue was $13.3 million, up 42.1%, and adjusted EBITDA was $2.4 million, up 31% from the prior year.
  • Extended Warranty segment revenue increased to $17.6 million, with adjusted EBITDA declining to $600,000.
  • Net debt at quarter end was $46.3 million, decreasing from $52 million at year-end, mainly due to PIPE proceeds.
  • The company recorded a $600,000 nonrecurring legal settlement expense.

Q&A

  • Christian Solberg, Sun Mountain Partners, asked if the reported $22 million to $23 million run rate EBITDA included recent acquisitions. Fitzgerald confirmed it did and explained the breakdown when subtracting contribution from new deals.
  • Solberg asked about the decline in run rate EBITDA excluding new acquisitions compared to Q1; Fitzgerald attributed the shortfall to "warranty had a pretty tough year-over-year comp on a GAAP basis."
  • James Carbonara, Hayden IR, asked if Kingsway would cease acquisitions after reaching its target. Fitzgerald responded, "No, I don't think that that's a good expectation...we currently have 2 OIRs actively looking for our next acquisition...we're not going to go pencils down once we hit some internal subjective target."
  • Carbonara inquired about OIR pipeline; Fitzgerald said, "we certainly expect this number to increase. We'd like to get back up to our kind of normal 4 to 5, and we have a great pipeline."
  • On acquisition evolution, Fitzgerald explained a "focus on revenue quality, with a higher standard of recurring revenue being primary."
  • Regarding OIR hiring, Fitzgerald stated, "we have a set of criteria, a set of attributes, that we think are important...we're going to continue to screen based on our criteria."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, with questions focused on acquisition pacing, financial metrics, and operational strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and responses, with Fitzgerald stating, "The energy at Kingsway is palpable, and I look forward to sharing more good news in the second half of 2025."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts exhibited increased confidence and engagement, reflecting the company's active acquisition strategy and capital raise.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a significant capital raise ($15.7 million PIPE) and an increase in the annual KSX acquisition target to 3–5, compared to 2–3 previously.
  • Five acquisitions year-to-date outpaced the pace discussed in Q1, signaling acceleration.
  • KSX run rate EBITDA rose to $22–23 million from $18–19 million, though excluding new acquisitions, the figure was lower due to Extended Warranty segment GAAP headwinds.
  • Analysts in the current quarter asked more detailed questions about acquisition strategy and run rate metrics, whereas the previous quarter focused more on platform strategy and leadership.
  • Management tone was more assertive and expansionary, reflecting increased momentum and capital resources.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that the Extended Warranty segment had a "pretty tough year-over-year comp on a GAAP basis," impacting run rate EBITDA.
  • Hansen discussed a $600,000 expense tied to a legacy legal settlement, clarifying it is nonrecurring.
  • Management emphasized discipline in acquisitions, aiming for quality over simply hitting targets.
  • No new material risks were highlighted beyond sector performance and legacy legal costs.

Final Takeaway

Kingsway closed the quarter with a strengthened balance sheet, a higher annual acquisition target, and a record level of KSX segment earnings power. The company executed three acquisitions post-PIPE, reinforced its search fund strategy, and signaled ongoing acquisition activity for the remainder of 2025. Management described the second quarter as a major inflection point, with momentum building across platforms and a clear focus on scaling through disciplined, recurring-revenue acquisitions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SoftBank acquires Foxconn’s Ohio EV facility to potentially boost Stargate project
data.description:
SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) (OTCPK:SFTBF) is the buyer taking ownership of Foxconn Technology's (OTCPK:FXCOF) electric vehicle plant in Ohio, a move intended to jump-start the company’s $500B Stargate data center project with OpenAI and Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), Bloomberg News reported.

SoftBank had approached the Apple (AAPL) supplier — which is formally known as Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) — with its plan to establish data centers and related infrastructure across the U.S., the report added, citing people with knowledge of the matter. The EV plant sale is a result of these efforts by the Japanese company.

Foxconn and SoftBank did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Earlier this week, Hon Hai Precision Industry, the flagship unit of Foxconn, said it agreed to sell the EV plant to Crescent Dune LLC for $375M without disclosing the company behind the entity. SoftBank is Foxconn’s counterparty in the transaction, the report noted.

Foxconn would operate the plant and use the site for its own AI server manufacturing business, the report added. The Ohio facility may be used to host a data center.

The two companies, which collaborated to make SoftBank’s humanoid Pepper robots a decade ago, formed a joint venture this year to establish a data center and manufacturing venture in the U.S., with each investing $735M. It is not clear if SoftBank’s investment in the venture includes the $375M for the EV plant, the report added.

On Thursday, SoftBank's CFO Yoshimitsu Goto said it was taking longer than expected to get the Stargate project off the ground, acknowledging for the first time that the $500B AI collaboration in the U.S. with OpenAI is slowing down.

SoftBank's Founder Masayoshi Son and OpenAI Co-Founder Sam Altman introduced the Stargate project in January along with President Donald Trump, with promises to start deploying $100B “immediately” and raise that to about $500B.

Meanwhile, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed OpenAI has proceeded with other data centers that use the Stargate brand. A project in Abilene, Texas, touted as Stargate’s first site, was on-track before Trump’s announcement or SoftBank’s involvement. The Japanese company was not involved in plans for a large Stargate data center in Norway, where OpenAI would be the anchor customer. OpenAI’s Stargate project also includes a 5-gigawatt data center complex in Abu Dhabi.

data.title: AMC Networks' streaming revenue contributes to Q2 beat
data.description:
AMC Networks (NASDAQ:AMCX) swung to a profit in the second quarter,  fueled by double digit increases in content licensing revenue, ad revenue, and streaming revenue, driving unadjusted income to $0.91 per share versus a loss of $0.66 in the same quarter last year.

On an adjusted basis, the company earned a profit of $0.69 per share, which was down 44% from a year ago but 8 cents above expectations. Net revenue was also down from a year ago to $600M but exceeded estimates by $17M.

The results drove shares higher in Friday’s premarket action with shares last traded with a gain of 2%.

“In the second quarter, we saw streaming revenue growth accelerate, strength in content licensing and continued healthy free cash flow generation. We are increasing our free cash flow outlook for 2025 and now expect approximately $250 million of free cash flow for the full year,” said Kristin Dolan, CEO of AMC Networks.

In the company’s domestic markets, a 2% increase in streaming subscriptions contributed to a 12% increase in streaming revenue. Subscription revenue and affiliate revenue were both down, however, by 1% and 12%, respectively, offset by a 26% improvement in content licensing revenue, resulting in a 2% decline in domestic revenue.

International revenue fell 16% due to lower subscription and advertising revenue.

On the balance sheet, AMC Networks (NASDAQ:AMCX) generated $95.7 million in free cash flow for the quarter, up 0.6% year-over-year. The company expects $250M of free cash flow for FY25.
